title: Florida weather modification law — May 9 2026
narrative: Florida's Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) received 20,000+ complaints from citizens reporting alleged weather modification/chemtrail activity since Governor Ron DeSantis signed Senate Bill 56 (2025), banning weather modification as a third-degree felony. DEP has found ZERO evidence of any violations. Officials claim citizens are confusing ordinary contrails with secret geoengineering programs.
telemetry: Federal law (50 USC) REQUIRES any weather modification operator to file advance reports with NOAA at least 10 days before activities. There is NO federal filing record for any active weather modification in Florida since SB 56 took effect.

DeSantis publicly mocked constituents raising chemtrail concerns as having "kooky ideas about blocking the sun" — a rhetorical dismissal that preceded a law specifically targeting the very practice they feared.

DEP's own spokesperson acknowledged the reports reflect "a common and understandable misconception between naturally occurring clouds or aircraft condensation trails... and intentional weather modification activity." This admits the phenomenon being reported (contrails) IS real and visible — what officials frame as misidentified.

No company has been charged, fined, or investigated under SB 56. No equipment has been seized. The law produced a felony category for an activity that demonstrably isn't happening — or isn't being detected.
Cross-reference: The NOAA Weather Modification Clearinghouse database showed ZERO authorized weather modification projects in Florida for 2025.
analysis:
Official sources say one thing. The evidence says another. The law created a felony offense for an activity for which there is no evidence — while actual atmospheric phenomena (contrails) are being visibly emitted by aircraft at unprecedented rates due to increased commercial aviation traffic. Who drafted SB 56, who lobbied for it, and does its existence serve to discredit legitimate citizen observation of the sky — or provide cover for undisclosed atmospheric activity not covered by federal reporting requirements?
